title: Relate Area to Addition (Tiling and Arrays)
essential_question: How is finding area connected to addition?

pretest.question: A rectangle has 4 rows of 5 unit squares. Use repeated addition: 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = ?

learn.title: Area as Repeated Addition
learn.content: When you cover a rectangle with unit squares, each ROW has the same number of squares. Adding the squares row by row is REPEATED ADDITION. Example: 4 rows of 5 squares each: 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 20.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.4 'Relate Area to Addition'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic D — Area and Addition
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Repeated Addition Becomes Multiplication
learn.content: Repeated addition is the same as multiplication. 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 4 × 5 = 20. Multiplication is just a faster way to write repeated addition.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.4 'Relate Area to Addition'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic D — Area and Addition
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Arrays of Unit Squares
learn.content: An ARRAY is a rectangular arrangement of items in equal rows. The unit squares of a rectangle form an array. Counting an array by rows and adding gives the total = AREA.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.4 'Relate Area to Addition'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic D — Area and Addition
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Adding Areas of Two Rectangles
learn.content: If a shape can be split into two rectangles (no overlap), find each area separately, then ADD them together. Total area = Area 1 + Area 2.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.4 'Relate Area to Addition'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic D — Area and Addition
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Why Adding Works
learn.content: Two rectangles side by side don't share unit squares. So if Rectangle 1 has 12 squares and Rectangle 2 has 8 squares, the total is 12+8=20 squares. Adding works whenever there are no overlaps.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.4 'Relate Area to Addition'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic D — Area and Addition
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Tiling a Rectangle
learn.content: TILING means covering a rectangle with unit squares (or square tiles) without gaps or overlaps. Tiling helps us see why area = rows × columns and why repeated addition works.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.4 'Relate Area to Addition'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic D — Area and Addition
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Choose Your Tool
learn.content: (1) Repeated addition: best for small numbers or when learning. (2) Multiplication: faster, especially for larger rectangles. (3) Split + add: best for irregular shapes that can be cut into rectangles.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.4 'Relate Area to Addition'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic D — Area and Addition
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Lesson Summary
learn.content: Today: (1) Area can be found by repeated addition (row + row + row...). (2) Repeated addition = multiplication. (3) Split irregular shapes into rectangles, find each area, then ADD. (4) Tiling is the visual model.

practice_r3.question: A composite shape: 4×5 + 3×5 (side by side, sharing one edge). Total area?
solution_steps: Step 1: Sharing an edge means the two rectangles don't overlap — they just touch.
